So, how has my experience been with the iPhone 17 after using it for about a month? Honestly, it's been pretty great. The Pro models use the A19 Pro, while the base model runs on the A19. But for 99% of people, this phone is more than enough. is perfect for anyone who wants a great display, solid battery life, and flagship level performance at a more reasonable price. The iPhone 17 Pro is for those who want the absolute best camera experience for photos and videos and don't mind paying extra for it. But as someone who's used the iPhone 15 Pro as their daily driver for the past 2 years, I can honestly say switching to the base iPhone 17 has been seamless. To wrap things up, should you upgrade to the iPhone 17 if you're coming from the iPhone 16 or an older model? In my opinion, yes. Even compared to last year's iPhone 16, the iPhone 17 offers meaningful improvements in performance, display quality, and overall user experience. It's faster, smoother, and feels more refined across the board, making it one of the most balanced iPhone Apple has released in years. I also think the flexible front camera with its almost square sensor is really clever. The maximum focus distance of the main camera could be improved, the full-screen brightness is somewhat lower, storage upgrades are extremely expensive, and iOS 26 is still struggling with minor bugs, not to mention the current, still limited, integration of AI features. In comparison, the Pixel 10 Pro features a super bright display and a significantly better focus limit for the main camera.  But in my opinion, its greatest strength is the software. Clean Android 16 with countless useful AI features such as Gemini Live, Live Call Translation or Circle to Search.  8K video capabilities and a good, though not outstanding, battery life are also advantages .  The Tensor G5 chip used may not be in the top league in terms of performance, but it is fast enough for everyday use.  Criticism is leveled at the camera's sometimes inconsistent color calibration and the sluggish UFS 3.1 storage in the base version.  Incomprehensible for a flagship model in this price range. Ultimately, both could be a little faster when charging , but all in all, these are two extremely sophisticated smartphones, each playing to its own strengths .  The iPhone with its performance and ecosystem, the Pixel with its clever AI features.  For true battery monsters, you should opt for the larger Pixel 10 Pro XL or iPhone 17 Pro Max. title: Das beste iPhone für wirklich jeden? - iPhone 17 (review)
transcript: The regular iPhone.  For years, it has always been the iPhone model that felt most neglected by Apple.  Here, compromises had to be made, and even then, it still had to be very expensive . This year, however, everything could be different with the iPhone 17 [music], because the price has remained the same and there are some new features.  First and foremost, a huge thank you goes out once again to notebooksbilliger.de, who provided me with the iPhone 17 and without whom this video would not have been possible [music].  So be sure to check out notebooksbilliger.de [music] next time you're buying tech.  Basically, the design language has n't changed at all compared to its predecessor, except that the glass used in the iPhone 17 is significantly more robust, although I couldn't really test that , you still get a case consisting of a matte glass back, which is again available in various beautiful colors, and a matte aluminum frame, on which, among other things, the Camera Control Button is located, which you can use to control the camera.  I [music] myself am not really convinced by this button .  Because everything is essentially the same as its predecessor in terms of materials and form factor, the iPhone 17 feels exactly the same in the hand as the iPhone 16, which is a good thing, because the iPhone 16 was already one of the smartphones that felt really good in the hand in my opinion.  Above all, I am also a big fan of the form factor, which in my eyes is perfect for a smartphone.  But let me briefly talk about the design again, because there's one more thing that I liked about the predecessor and, logically, also about the iPhone 17: the design of the camera element.  You still receive two cameras that are still positioned one above the other, which results in the new camera element being less wide and more elongated.  The only disadvantage of this camera element as well. The individual lenses are once again not protected, and consequently, they can scratch very quickly.  Furthermore, they protrude very far from the casing, causing both the iPhone 17 and the iPhone 16 to wobble considerably on the table.  What the iPhone 17 absolutely must have is an IP certification, which is of course also offered here. It has an IP68 certification, which protects the iPhone at depths of up to 6 meters for 30 minutes.  Before I move on to the next point, I would like to address two things that have actually been known for a long time, but are always important to me.  Firstly, the vibration motor is, as is typical for Apple, simply top-notch and feels super high-quality in every situation .  And secondly, the built-in speakers, as is to be expected, do not disappoint in any way and are still the best in a smartphone.  Since there is only one iPhone 17 model this year and no Plus variant, there is logically only one display size.  It measures 6.3 inches and the OLED display itself has a PPI of 460 and a maximum brightness of 3000 nits.  So everything is exactly as you would expect from an iPhone in 2025.  Oh yes, and I can hardly believe I'm finally saying this , but the iPhone 17 is finally the first regular iPhone model with a 120Hz display. I've completely lost track of how long we've been waiting for this .  Now, finally, every current iPhone comes with a high refresh rate.  The display is only interrupted by the familiar dynamic island, which has the functions that have been known for a few iPhone generations now.  Now, a few words about the front design itself .  The display bezels have become significantly thinner and are, logically, symmetrical again.  In general, the front design of the iPhone 17 is among the most modern on the market.  Importantly, Face ID is of course still on board, with the necessary sensors located in the dynamic island, ensuring that what I believe is the most intuitive locking method has once again found its way into the iPhone 17 .  The locking process is totally intuitive and works really well, and therefore I will probably always remain a big fan of Face ID .  The iPhone 17 is equipped with the A19 Bionic chip.  It also has 8 GB of RAM, the same amount as last year.  I would be lying if I said that you can feel a drastic performance difference compared to the iPhone 16, but logically, the iPhone 17's performance is also flawless.  Just so you have a comparison.  The iPhone 16 scored 3311 points in the single-core benchmark test.  and the iPhone 17 at 3592. In the multi-color test, the iPhone 16 then scored and the iPhone 17 at 9116 points.  So there has been an increase in the values, but it is not drastic and therefore not really noticeable in everyday life.  There is simply nothing to complain about, and I would even go so far as to say that we have now reached a point with smartphones, or at least since this year, where virtually every smartphone offers good performance, and there are only nuances in very specific cases where one smartphone performs better than another.  In everyday use, everything runs smoothly without the slightest bugs or anything like that.  Apps open quickly and stay open in the background for a very long time .  It's simply always a pleasant feeling to know that with an iPhone you get performance that you don't have to worry about for years to come .  As befits a new iPhone model , it also features the latest iOS version, and this is probably the biggest one in a long time.  It's iOS 26, and this version hasn't only received positive feedback, because with iOS 26 and the new Liquid Glass design, everything about the software has become, as the name suggests, more glass-like, playful, and transparent. Many elements now really look like glass or liquid glass and behave like it too.  For my part, I have to say that I really like most of it and it also fits the iPhones really well.  However, not everything is perfect, and there are still occasional glitches, display errors, and elements that are really difficult to see or read in some situations. Apple definitely needs to address this as quickly as possible and improve one or two things.  But if that happens, and I'm sure it will, and faster than we think, then I really have to say that I 'm a fan of iOS 26, even though it 's obviously a very drastic update.  However, what I 'm still not really a fan of is Apple Intelligence, and that's why I do n't want to waste too much time here .  While some progress has been made , we still have to wait, for example, for Siri to finally become more intelligent.  What Google and Samsung have been accomplishing for some time now is far more impressive.  With the iPhone 17, I usually didn't have to worry about the battery at all , because I got through a day without any problems and still had about 30% battery left in the evening, and that was with screen ontime values ​​of up to 4 to 5 hours [with music] .  When I spent almost the entire day using Wi-Fi, it was sometimes even a little better.  So I definitely ca n't complain here, even though it could be better and the battery life isn't the best of the best. Even though you can charge in three ways – wireless, Macfe and USB-C – I have to say it's the latter that disappointed me, because the charging speed itself is still rather mediocre.  If you leave the iPhone charging for a full 30 minutes, you'll only get about 55% battery charge.  These are other smartphones, or rather, manufacturers have been significantly further ahead for a long time now.  The iPhone 17 features a dual camera system, which includes a 48- megapixel main camera with an f/16 aperture and a new, sharper 48- megapixel ultra-wide camera with an f/22 aperture. First, the general question: Is there any difference at all compared to the results of the predecessor?  And here it must be said that with the main camera this is practically never the case, only in very specific situations with the ultrawide-angle lens, simply due to the higher number of megapixels, then here and there, especially in poorer lighting conditions.  But let's now turn to the individual cameras. The colors in the images are definitely impressive, and the dynamic range and sharpness are simply top-notch.  There is also the option to zoom twice with the main sensor, which crops the 24 megapixel photos to 12 megapixels, resulting in images that still look incredibly good and have the same sharpness as the full 12 megapixel main sensor from the iPhone 14, so I also liked to use the 2x zoom here and there. However, I used the ultra-wide-angle camera more.  Both the results during the day itself and the very wide field of view are still simply top-notch.  Even in poor lighting conditions, thanks to the new 48 megapixels, the results are really good and have noticeably improved compared to the iPhone 16. So, you could say that the iPhone 17 offers a solid smartphone camera that makes taking photos really fun. Of course, you can also record videos with the iPhone 17, and in 4K at up to 60 FPS.  The cinema and action modes are of course also available again. I still really enjoy the videos. It really hasn't been a long year with the iPhone where I've said that the regular iPhone is the best choice for everyone. [music] But this year seems to be one of those years, because with the iPhone 17 you basically get everything you could wish for and have long hoped for from a normal iPhone.  Finally, you no longer have to compromise on the display and really feel like you're using a smartphone from 2025. The processor, typical of Apple, is once again at an extremely high level and will not let you down.  And the fact that the camera, especially the ultra-wide-angle camera, has been explicitly improved makes the iPhone a good choice in many respects .  Especially since the price hasn't increased and you can get the iPhone 17 with 256 GB for €949.  So if you're looking for the perfect time to buy a regular iPhone, now is probably the right time.

title: Apple iPhone 17 Pro Max Production Cost تكلفة انتاج ايفون ١٧ برو ماكس
transcript: How much do you think Apple costs to make the iPhone 17 Pro Max ?  Let me tell you, the aluminum body costs $20, and the battery costs $4. Imagine if they spent $10 on the battery, how much battery would that be? The 256GB storage costs $20, the RAM costs $21, and the modem that handles Wi-Fi and 4G is $90. The iPhone screen costs $80, the processor or chip is $90, and the cameras are $80. So, the entire iPhone 17 Pro Max costs Apple $408. Apple sells this phone for $1200, meaning they make a 200% profit on just one phone.

title: Tesla Pi Phone 2026: 5 Shocking Feature That Can Beat Iphone 17. What's Hidden Behind?
transcript: Smartphones today keep getting faster, thinner, and flashier, but weaker where it really matters. The batteries don't last. The glass cracks too easily. And every update seems designed to slow you down or track what you do. Elon Musk doesn't build things that way. Every product he touches, from Tesla cars to SpaceX rockets, follows one rule: independence, durability, and freedom from control. Now, rumors say the Tesla Pi Phone 2026 brings that same philosophy into the smartphone world. Built not as a gadget, but as a machine made to last and made to protect its user, not the company behind it. So, what kind of phone does that create? Could it finally be the first device that won't die after 2 years? Or one that actually puts you, not Apple, not Google, in control of your data? We'll break it all down in today's video. You're watching Auto Gear Shift, where we don't just chase Tesla rumors, we decode them. If you love real engineering talk and want to understand what Musk is really planning next, hit subscribe and ring the bell. Let's push this channel past 12,531 subs so we can keep uncovering what others won't. Feature number one. Can a phone really survive 20 years? The Tesla PYphone 2026 doesn't use the same soft aluminum shell you find on today's flagships. Instead, its frame is forged from a Titan aluminum matrix composite, a proprietary alloy derived from the 30X stainless and aluminum hybrid used in the Cybert truck's exoskeleton and SpaceX Falcon booster frames. Laboratory tests suggest this material achieves a yield strength of over 800 megapascals, roughly 40% higher than Apple's 7,000 series aluminum and nearly double that of conventional smartphone housings made from 6000 series alloys. This alloy isn't just strong, it's smart. By skipping paint or color coding, Tesla allows the surface to naturally generate a microscopic oxide layer when scratched. that oxidation acts like armor, sealing micro fractures and preventing corrosion. According to aerospace engineers familiar with the material, this process lets the metal retain 98% of its tensile integrity after 10,000 scratch abrasion cycles, a feat no commercial smartphone frame has achieved to date. Temperature resilience is another gamecher. Where most aluminum phones begin to soften at around 250° C, the Titan aluminum matrix maintains its rigidity up to 420° C, ensuring the chassis stays stable even under direct heat exposure or rapid cold to hot transitions. Something SpaceX already validated for its orbital components. For many older practical users, this isn't just an engineering flex. It's peace of mind. A phone that doesn't crack, warp, or fade means no yearly upgrade cycle. No fragile beauty that dies in 2 years. It's built to endure. And reportedly, it's the first smartphone ever certified under SpaceX's aerospace structural grade standard, making the PY phone less a gadget and more a precision machine for life. What do you think? Comment one if you'd trade your iPhone for a Tesla Pi phone. Comment too if you still trust Apple's ecosystem more and tell us why in the comments below. We're reading every thought. If this feature hasn't persuaded you yet, just wait for the other four coming. Feature number two. Can a smartphone battery really last 25 years without fading? Tesla seems determined to prove that battery death, the silent killer of every modern device, can finally be eliminated. The PY phone 2026 introduces a solidstate power cell derived from the same prototype battery packs being tested in the Tesla Model 2. Unlike conventional lithium ion cells filled with flammable liquid electrolyte, Tesla's design replaces it with a ceramic polymer solid electrolyte capable of withstanding temperatures up to 350° C without structural breakdown. This new chemistry packs a volutric energy density of 1,50 W hours per liter compared to around 650 W hours per liter in current iPhone 17 cells, a 1.6 times increase in stored energy per cm. More impressive is its endurance. The cell has been laboratory tested to retain over 88% capacity after 10,000 full charge discharge cycles, which equates to roughly 25 to 27 years of daily use. For comparison, a lithium ion pack typically degrades below 80% after just 500 cycles or less than 2 years for heavy users. charging speed borders on unbelievable, but is backed by engineering logic. Using a dualphase modulated current protocol adapted from Tesla Energy's Power Wall 4 architecture, the PY phone can reach 80% charge in 150 seconds and 100% in just under 5 minutes, drawing a peak input of 180 W at 98% conversion efficiency. The solid electrolyte allows higher thermal tolerance, meaning the pack remains below 42° C even at full speed charging, far below the critical 60° C threshold that typically accelerates cell decay. Feature number three, can a phone really stay online without any signal? The Tesla PYphone 2026 is rumored to break one of the final barriers in mobile communication, total independence from cell towers. Unlike the iPhone 17, which still relies entirely on carrier networks and offers only a limited satellite SOS feature for emergencies, the PY phone integrates direct Starlink satellite connectivity for full continuous data service anywhere on Earth. At the heart of this system is a 0.8 8 mm ceramic phased array antenna embedded just beneath the Tesla logo capable of dynamically tracking up to 12 Starlink satellites simultaneously in low Earth orbit at 550 km altitude. This design gives the device a beam forming accuracy of 0.2° ensuring stable contact even during movement or partial obstruction something current smartphone antennas cannot achieve. In standard testing by Starlink engineers, prototype PY phones demonstrated downlink speeds averaging 35 to 50 megabits per second and uplink speeds around 20 megabits per second with a latency of just 45 to 55 milliseconds. That's roughly four times faster and six times lower latency than Apple's satellite SOS function, which is capped at 8 megabits per second and limited to textbased emergency transmission. Tesla's model supports real-time voice calls, encrypted messaging, live navigation, and continuous data sync even in areas completely devoid of cellular signal. Power consumption, traditionally a problem with satellite comms, is handled by a low loss GN amplifier module that reduces energy draw by 32% compared to conventional RF front ends, allowing up to 9 hours of continuous Starlink connectivity on a full charge. Feature number four, can a smartphone truly be private? Unlike the iPhone 17, which still depends on Qualcomm's Snapdragon X70 modem and Apple's cloud-l Siri processing, Tesla has cut out every third-party dependency. At its core lies the Dojo T1, a compact derivative of Tesla's in-house Dojo AI training chip, redesigned for mobile use. Built on a 5 nanometer architecture and capable of 18 trillion operations per second, it handles all computation, voice, image, and biometric data directly on the device. Running on Tesla OS, a closed loop operating system forked from Tesla's vehicle firmware. The Pi phone doesn't transmit a single bite of personal data to the cloud. There's no advertising framework, no telemetry logging, and no external API access without explicit user consent. All machine learning, from facial recognition to voice commands, happens locally through the Dojo T1's AI cores, which reduces latency by 43% compared to Apple's neural engine while completely eliminating server-based tracking. But the most controversial feature isn't software, it's hardware. On the left frame sits a physical privacy switch, a mechanical toggle that severs the power lines to the camera, microphone, GPS, and modem simultaneously. When switched to private mode, these circuits are physically disconnected from the mainboard, not just disabled by code. No app, process, or even Tesla itself can reactivate them until the user flips the switch back. For older users, those who've lived through decades of data breaches, spam calls, and silent surveillance, this isn't a gimmick. It's reassurance. A phone that can't spy because its sensors are literally offline speaks to a deeper engineering truth. Tesla isn't selling privacy as a slogan. It's building it into the hardware. Feature number five, can a phone really cool itself like an engine? Overheating is the silent killer of modern smartphones, draining performance, aging components, and turning even top tier flagships into throttled hand warmers. The Tesla PYphone 2026 takes a page straight out of its automotive lineage, introducing an ultra thermal cooling system based on the same liquid vapor plate graphine architecture that regulates temperature inside the Model S battery pack. At the core of this design lies a vacuumsealed vapor chamber connected to a micro liquid circuit that runs through the phone's titanium aluminum frame. Heat generated by the Dojo T1 CPU and Tesla's onboard GPU cores is instantly transferred into this chamber where it's spread across graphine capillaries and dissipated evenly through the chassis. Internal testing data from Tesla Labs shows that under sustained 8K video recording or 100% AI workload, the PY phone's core temperature remains below 35° C, while the iPhone 17 Pro, under similar conditions, often peaks at 46 to 48° C before thermal throttling kicks in. The engineering difference is more than comfort. It's longevity. Lower thermal stress means up to 45% slower transistor degradation, 30% higher battery stability, and consistent frame rates even after hours of continuous use. More impressively, the system is rated to operate in extreme environments, maintaining full performance from -20° C to 60° C, conditions that would most consumer devices. For practical users, especially men over 50 who prioritize reliability over specs, this isn't just cooling, it's assurance. A phone that doesn't overheat, doesn't throttle, and doesn't fail early is one you can actually trust. So after all five features, aerospace grade metal, 25ear solidate battery, Starlink connection, hardware privacy, and engine style cooling, the Tesla PY phone 2026 sounds less like a phone and more like a survival machine built for the next two decades. Now, I want to hear from you. Comment yes, PY phone if you think the Pi phone could really replace the iPhone 17. If you enjoyed this breakdown, don't forget to like, subscribe, and turn on the bell so you won't miss our next deep dive into what Elon Musk might be secretly building next. See you again next time.
